findings.description: pickle.loads on bytes read from Redis (L2) is CWE-502 untrusted deserialization: an attacker able to write the cache value (exposed/unauthenticated Redis, co-tenant, or cache-poisoning path) achieves arbitrary code execution via a crafted __reduce__ payload; replace pickle with a non-executable codec such as JSON or msgpack, or add HMAC integrity verification over the stored blob.

set() serializes with pickle.dumps, establishing the pickle wire format that makes the line-60 deserialization exploitable; the safe fix must change both the writer and reader to a non-code-executing serialization format.

_is_expired returns 'age < self.ttl' (inverted), so fresh L1 entries are evicted while stale ones are served forever; security-relevant because TTL-based eviction never fires for aged data, prolonging service of stale/should-be-invalidated cached values (deferring core logic detail to sibling critics).

pickle.loads (and the surrounding get path) has no try/except, so a corrupt or maliciously malformed Redis value raises an unhandled exception on every read of that key, an availability/DoS concern subsumed by the deserialization finding.
